serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw sardines is one medium fish weighing approximately 38g edible portion, commonly grilled, baked, or pan-fried before consumption.

nutrient_source.title: Sardines, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sardines, raw (cr_id F03010005). Saturated fat value cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 (Sardine, Atlantic, raw) which reports saturated fat 0.69g/100g; rounded to 0.7g/100g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and select micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 where HPB does not report these fields.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sardines, raw contain 0.0g carbohydrate per 100g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate content is zero, yielding a calculated GL of zero regardless of GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 665mg/100g exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods; however, this reflects natural marine sodium and is partially offset by excellent potassium (352mg/100g) and calcium (725mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive individuals should consume raw sardines without added salt, prepared by grilling or baking, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to leverage the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio; portion size should be moderated to limit total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate (0.0g/100g) and zero sugar (0.0g/100g) result in no glycaemic impact; high protein (19.7g/100g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw sardines are an excellent protein source for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to their zero carbohydrate content and high-quality protein; avoid frying in batter or serving with high-GI accompaniments to maintain glycaemic benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA; polyunsaturated fat 1.05g/100g), low saturated fat (0.7g/100g), and high calcium (725mg/100g); omega-3s reduce triglycerides, lower inflammation, and support endothelial function.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw sardines are cardioprotective when grilled or baked without added salt; their omega-3 fatty acid content reduces cardiovascular risk, but the naturally high sodium (665mg/100g) requires portion control and salt-free preparation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) directly suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-6; very high calcium (725mg/100g) supports bone health in patients on corticosteroids.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sardines are recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to their anti-inflammatory omega-3 content and high calcium; prepare by grilling, baking, or steaming without added salt to preserve omega-3 integrity and avoid excess sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sardines, raw are fresh uncooked small marine oily fish belonging to the family Clupeidae, characterised by a blue-green back, silver belly, and greyish-pink flesh with a tender, oily texture. They are found predominantly in the Atlantic Ocean and Mediterranean Sea and are widely consumed across global cuisines including Mediterranean, Southeast Asian, and Portuguese cooking traditions. Raw sardines are typically prepared by grilling, baking, pan-frying, or steaming; they are rarely consumed raw without cooking in most culinary traditions. Nutritionally, raw sardines are exceptionally rich in high-quality protein (19.7g/100g), contain very low total fat (2.9g/100g) with a favourable fatty acid profile including omega-3 EPA and DHA, and provide outstanding calcium (725mg/100g) and iron (3.95mg/100g). They contain zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making them ideal for low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, and diabetic dietary patterns. Sodium is naturally elevated at 665mg/100g due to marine origin, which requires consideration for hypertensive individuals. Sardines are one of the most clinically recommended oily fish for cardiovascular protection, anti-inflammatory benefit in rheumatoid arthritis, and bone health support. Their omega-3 content reduces triglycerides and systemic inflammation, while their calcium density is comparable to dairy products. Energy density is low at 106 kcal/100g, supporting weight management goals.
common_user_queries: Can I eat sardines if I have high blood pressure?
Are raw sardines high in omega-3?
How much protein is in sardines?
Is sardine good for heart health?
What is the calcium content of sardines?
How many calories in raw sardines?
meal_context: served with rice or bread as a main meal

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03010005, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid profile cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 (Sardine, Atlantic, raw). Both approved primary sources are in close agreement. No estimation or analogy required for core values.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as sardines are a pure animal protein and fat source containing no carbohydrates. Sugar_g is naturally zero by the same basis. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contain no dietary fibre. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 confirmed per USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088. Sardines, raw; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0g/100g). glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel per template requirements with gi_category='not_applicable' to signal inapplicability; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as the mathematical product of GI and zero carbohydrate content yields zero. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record. While USDA FoodData Central does report some of these values for Atlantic sardines, the HPB record is the primary source and these supplementary values were not incorporated to avoid source mixing without explicit cross-validation. Follow-up enrichment from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15088 is recommended for a future iteration. Sodium value of 665mg/100g reflects natural marine sodium content of raw sardines and is not attributable to added salt. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input health_suitability contained an internal inconsistency (stating sardines are suitable due to omega-3 while simultaneously marking suitable=false); the output health_context corrects this to concern_level=low reflecting the positive clinical evidence for omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name 'Sardines, raw'. No local-language mandatory requirement applies as this is a generic global marine food item, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish requiring mandatory native-script names under G6.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g=0.0 (naturally zero, documented), sodium_mg=665.0, saturated_fat_g=0.7 (from USDA FoodData Central cross-reference), sugar_g=0.0 (naturally zero, documented). All four required secondary fields present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index=0 (numeric sentinel value), gi_category='not_applicable', glycemic_load_per_serving=0.0 (numeric), gi_source present. Food contains 0.0g carbohydrate per 100g; GI is not applicable. The numeric value 0 satisfies the template requirement for a numeric field while gi_category='not_applicable' signals the clinical inapplicability. glycemic_load_per_serving=0.0 is mathematically correct as GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g x serving_g) / 100 = 0 when carbohydrate_g=0.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03411433, sardine whole or parts), FoodEx2 code A00LQ (Sardines) provided, SNOMED CT code 227144007 (Sardine - food substance) provided. All three anchors are defensible and consistent with the food identity.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters). Covers food identity, preparation methods, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients including omega-3, calcium, protein, sodium, and energy density.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (concern_level=high, sodium-driven), Type 2 Diabetes (concern_level=low, zero carbohydrate), Cardiovascular Disease (concern_level=low, omega-3 benefit). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name='Type 2 Diabetes' correctly used.
